name: Oscar Pettiford
abstract: Oscar Pettiford (September 30, 1922 – September 8, 1960) was an American jazz double bassist and composer. He was one of the earliest musicians to work in the bebop idiom.

concerts.label: Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic
Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic
Woody Herman and His Orchestra and The Nat King Cole Trio

works_created.t: Swingin' till the Girls Come Home

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: I said, 'How would you like to come with the band.' ... And we started out and everything was fine.

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Oscar Pettiford was the bass player... First time he did it, with our sextet.
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: They had a great group. They had Sir Charles Thompson on piano, and Oscar Pettiford playing the bass, and Denzil Best on drums. ... So he asked me, he said, 'Why don't you come on and hook up with me, and switch the tenor?'

about_quotes.speaker: Budd Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: I started to working with Dizzy Gillespie and Oscar Pettiford... Dizzy and I said okay we'll stick together... Oscar Pettiford felt that he wanted to go get his own group

about_quotes.speaker: Akiyoshi Toshiko
about_quotes.evidence: McKutchen, Mac, said, “Well, this is Toshiko Akiyoshi, a pianist,” and they said, “Oh, hi, excuse me, I have to warm up,”... And we had a little jam session.
about_quotes.doc_title: Akiyoshi Toshiko — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Wilbur Ware
about_quotes.evidence: Pettiford was coming in to meet me. He says, 'Hey, wait a minute; hold it -- what's your name?' ... Pettiford offered he bought this fiddle for me
about_quotes.doc_title: 68088_Wilbur B. Ware, Sr oral history interview
